@thefrozenfire @jimmy_wales @prestonjbyrne I know this is weird, but whether something is speech for First Amendment purposes does not depend on what it is but on what it is doing. If, and to the extent that, it is part of the process of humans exchanging thoughts and emotions, it's speech.

If I "distribute a Bible" by

@thefrozenfire @jimmy_wales @prestonjbyrne I know this is weird, but whether something is speech for First Amendment purposes does not depend on what it is but on what it is doing. If, and to the extent that, it is part of the process of humans exchanging thoughts and emotions, it's speech.

If I "distribute a Bible" by
@benrayfield You don't need any of the rights the licence grants you to use the software, only to distribute it or create derivative works.

@Eve777M Close, it's a mango daiquiri.

It reminds me of the smoky daiquiris that I used to make for my father. He'd ask me what kind of daiquiri it was and I'd say, "It's a hickory daiquiri, Doc!".

@thefrozenfire @jimmy_wales @prestonjbyrne Yes. In the Microsoft anti-trust case. In various ADA website cases. Also, it's just obvious from how 1A law works. If "code is speech" works, then "burning cloth is not speech" would have worked in Texas v. Johnson.
@GloriousEggroll The law doesn't put any restrictions at all on how the indication can be changed? Because if it does, that's verification.

@DietCoke_Esq Suppose you have to argue that your case isn't moot in front of the Supreme Court because you really want to set precedent. That clause lets you argue that you have not gotten all the relief the lower court could have given you no matter what the other side concedes or agrees to.
@pcfreak30 @GWHayduke97 @jimmy_wales How are they different in a way that's relevant to the argument that you can't compel code changes because code is speech?

@thefrozenfire @jimmy_wales @prestonjbyrne Exactly. That's why this argument is nonsense and courts reject it. Compelling functional changes in a functional code product is not like compelling expression in an argument or work of art for 1A purposes.

@peirce_alva @jimmy_wales @prestonjbyrne I don't see why any of that would have any bearing on the argument that compelling code is compelling speech, the very argument courts consistently reject when the government demands functional changes to a functional product.
@peirce_alva @jimmy_wales @prestonjbyrne What does that have to do with whether the government is compelling code and therefore compelling speech?
@MicahAlexKenda1 @jimmy_wales @prestonjbyrne How so? They're both functional products protected by copyright and the government is arguing the law demands functional changes.

@jimmy_wales @prestonjbyrne Isn't this the same argument Microsoft made, and every US court soundly rejected, when the government argued they had to make modifications to Windows to remove anti-competitive aspects such as bundling?

probability.last_reasoning: Slight increase in bear probability from 10% to 12% driven by: extreme fear environment persisting, all compound stress legs deteriorating, Warsh Fed Chair nomination suggesting potential hawkish policy shift, and RLUSD's 78% Ethereum concentration undermining XRPL-native settlement narrative. Slight increase in mid probability from 38% to 34% (reduction) offset by moving 2% from base to bear. Kraken Fed master account is structurally positive but hasn't changed the macro stress picture. Bull case unchanged — institutional infrastructure building continues but macro headwinds have intensified.

bear_case.score_reasoning: Moderate and rising. RLUSD's 78% Ethereum concentration directly challenges the XRPL settlement thesis. PermissionedDEX zero adoption at day 12 is early but concerning. ODL opacity entering month 18+ is a genuine red flag. Competing infrastructure continues advancing. Macro stress pre-loaded with Warsh nomination adding hawkish overhang.

competing_infrastructure.name: SWIFT GPI
competing_infrastructure.status_text: SWIFT continues upgrading network for 11,500 banks; no confirmed XRPL integration. GPI same-day settlement covers most institutional needs without blockchain bridge dependency.

competing_infrastructure.name: Visa B2B Connect
competing_infrastructure.status_text: Visa's bank-to-bank network operates at scale with existing compliance infrastructure. No public evidence of losing ground to XRPL-based alternatives in target corridors.

competing_infrastructure.name: JPMorgan Kinexys
competing_infrastructure.status_text: Kinexys (formerly Onyx) processes significant daily volume for institutional clients. JPMorgan's banking relationships and regulatory standing give it natural advantages over Ripple for Tier 1 bank adoption.

competing_infrastructure.name: BIS Project Nexus
competing_infrastructure.status_text: Multi-central-bank coordination continues without confirmed XRPL dependency. If Nexus achieves sovereign settlement interoperability independently, it displaces the core XRP bridge currency thesis.

competing_infrastructure.name: Ethereum Institutional
competing_infrastructure.status_text: RLUSD's own distribution tells the story — 78% lives on Ethereum, not XRPL. Ripple's own stablecoin choosing Ethereum as primary venue undermines the XRPL-native settlement narrative. Ethereum's institutional DeFi ecosystem is deeper and more liquid.

bear_case.odl_stagnation: ODL volume data has been opaque for 18+ months — if volumes were growing impressively, Ripple would likely publish them. Silence is not confirmation of growth.
bear_case.token_velocity_concern: No hold-time or velocity data available. Without evidence that XRP is being held as settlement collateral rather than immediately sold after bridge transactions, the token value capture mechanism remains unproven.

macro_headwinds.name: Global Recession Risk
macro_headwinds.status_text: Fear & Greed at 10 (extreme fear), compound stress pre-loaded with all legs deteriorating, Section 122 tariffs active. Recession reduces cross-border payment volumes — directly undermines ODL utility thesis.

macro_headwinds.name: Crypto Winter Signals
macro_headwinds.status_text: BTC dropped to $72K before bouncing to $74K. Extreme fear persisting. XRP at $1.46 is down significantly from recent highs. Not yet a confirmed winter but sentiment is severely depressed.

macro_headwinds.name: Regulatory Reversal Risk
macro_headwinds.status_text: Fairshake PAC wins and Kraken Fed access suggest pro-crypto momentum continues. Bank lobby pushback is notable but unlikely to reverse the structural shift under current administration.

macro_headwinds.name: Rate Hike Extension
macro_headwinds.status_text: Kevin Warsh Fed Chair nomination is a hawkish signal. If confirmed, could extend higher rates through 2026-2027, tightening liquidity and increasing the cost of institutional experimentation with new settlement infrastructure.

bear_case.bear_narrative: HOLD position with increased monitoring. Three kill switches escalated to WARNING: ODL data vacuum remains the single most corrosive threat to thesis credibility — the primary falsification criterion has zero verifiable data with 10 months remaining. Today's critical discovery: RLUSD distribution skew (78% Ethereum / 22% XRPL) reveals a structural divergence between Ripple-the-company succeeding and XRPL-the-settlement-layer validating, forcing a refinement of what this thesis actually requires to hold. The compound stress framework is pre-loaded with all three legs deteriorating toward ELEVATED, but XRP's +7% resilience during extreme BTC fear is a paradox worth monitoring — it either signals institutional decoupling or is noise that reverses tomorrow. The thesis is not broken but it is unfalsifiable in its current state, and the Q3 2026 ODL transparency deadline is now the critical path: if that passes without data, we are not monitoring an investment thesis — we are maintaining a belief system.
